Title: Foundations: how LLMs and tokens actually work
Why: Everything else — cost, context limits, prompting — makes sense once you understand tokens and how a model predicts text.

Steps.Text: Read a plain-English intro to how LLMs predict the next token.
Play with a tokenizer to see how text becomes tokens.
Understand context windows and why long chats cost more.

Title: Prompting well
Why: A clear prompt is the cheapest, fastest quality win you have. Most 'the AI is dumb' moments are prompt problems.

Steps.Text: Learn role/task framing, step-by-step instructions and output formatting.
Practice few-shot examples to lock in a format.
Try terse 'caveman' prompts and compare quality vs token cost.

Title: Context engineering & token optimization
Why: As soon as you build something real, cost and context limits bite. Knowing what to put in (and leave out) is the core skill.

Steps.Text: Learn prompt caching to cut cost on repeated large contexts.
Practice trimming/summarizing history in long sessions.
Route cheap tasks to small models, hard tasks to frontier models.

Title: RAG: answer over your own data
Why: The standard way to make a model use your private/up-to-date knowledge without retraining it.

Steps.Text: Understand embeddings and vector similarity search.
Build a minimal RAG pipeline: chunk -> embed -> retrieve -> answer.
Improve retrieval quality with reranking and contextual retrieval.

Title: Tool use & function calling
Why: The mechanism that lets a model do things — search, query a DB, run code — instead of just talking.

Steps.Text: Learn how to define tools and handle tool-call results.
Wire one real tool (e.g. a web search or DB query) end to end.
Handle errors and retries so a bad tool call doesn't break the run.

Title: Building agents & agentic loops
Why: Going from one-shot prompts to a loop that plans, acts and self-corrects is what unlocks real automation.

Steps.Text: Read Anthropic's guide to building effective agents.
Implement a ReAct-style loop: think -> act -> observe -> repeat.
Add a stopping condition and verification so the loop ends well.

Title: MCP: connect your agent to tools
Why: The emerging standard for plugging AI into your systems in a reusable way instead of one-off integrations.

Steps.Text: Read the Model Context Protocol intro.
Connect an existing MCP server (filesystem, GitHub) to a client.
Write a tiny MCP server exposing one of your own tools.

Title: Run local & open-weight models
Why: For privacy, offline use, cheap high-volume calls and experimentation, running your own model is a superpower.

Steps.Text: Install Ollama and run a small model locally.
Learn quantization (e.g. 4-bit GGUF) to fit your hardware.
Point a tool (Cline, a script) at your local model via its API.

Title: Evals: measure quality before you ship
Why: Without a test set you're tuning blind. Evals tell you whether a prompt/model change actually helped.

Steps.Text: Collect 10-20 real examples with expected outcomes.
Score model output automatically (exact match, rules, or an LLM judge).
Re-run evals on every prompt/model change to catch regressions.

Title: Pick the right coding tool & workflow
Why: Using Copilot where you needed an agent (or vice-versa) wastes time and tokens. Match the tool to the task.

Steps.Text: Use an IDE assistant (Copilot) for inline completions while typing.
Use a coding agent (Claude Code, Codex, Cline) for multi-file tasks that need to run tests.
Give agents project memory (a CLAUDE.md / rules file) so they stop re-learning your repo.

Title: Understanding AI model types & architectures
Why: Not all models are the same shape. Knowing base vs. instruct-tuned, dense vs. Mixture-of-Experts, and multimodal vs. text-only tells you what a given model can actually do for your use case before you waste time on the wrong one.

Steps.Text: Learn the difference between a base model, an instruct-tuned model, and a chat model.
Understand Mixture of Experts (MoE) and why it's used to scale models efficiently.
Learn what multimodal means (vision, audio, code) versus text-only, and where each fits.

Title: AI security: prompt injection & agent risks
Why: The moment an agent can browse the web, read email, or run code, it inherits a new attack surface - untrusted content it reads can try to hijack its instructions. This is the top real-world risk in agentic AI today.

Steps.Text: Understand direct vs. indirect prompt injection and how each actually works.
Learn why agents need a clear boundary between trusted instructions (the user) and untrusted content (web pages, tool results, documents).
Review real prompt-injection incidents and how they were mitigated.

Title: AI ethics, safety & responsible use
Why: Understanding hallucination, bias, and where AI shouldn't be trusted unsupervised keeps you from shipping something that quietly does harm - this matters as much for a side project as it does at enterprise scale.

Steps.Text: Learn what hallucination is, why it happens, and why evals matter.
Understand how bias in training data shows up in model output.
Know where human review is still required - medical, legal, financial, and safety-critical decisions.

Title: Cybersecurity fundamentals for developers
Why: Every developer ships code that touches the internet. Basic security hygiene - auth, secrets, dependencies - prevents the large majority of real-world breaches, AI-powered or not.

Steps.Text: Learn the OWASP Top 10 web vulnerabilities.
Practice secrets management - never commit API keys; use environment variables or a vault.
Learn to reason about your own attack surface: what's actually reachable from the internet.

Title: Cloud & DevOps fundamentals
Why: Modern apps - AI-powered or not - live in containers on someone's cloud. Knowing the basics of what you're actually deploying to saves hours of confusion and avoids nasty billing surprises.

Steps.Text: Learn what a container is and why Docker/Kubernetes exist.
Understand the difference between IaaS, PaaS, and serverless.
Learn FinOps basics - why cloud bills spiral, and how teams keep them in check.

Title: Data engineering fundamentals
Why: RAG, fine-tuning, and analytics all live or die on data quality. Knowing how data actually moves - pipelines, lakes, fabrics - explains why 'garbage in, garbage out' is real, not a cliché.

Steps.Text: Learn the difference between a data warehouse, a data lake, and a data fabric.
Understand ETL/ELT pipelines at a conceptual level.
Learn why data governance - lineage, access control - matters once data feeds production AI systems.

Title: Quantum computing basics
Why: Not useful for your day job yet, but worth understanding conceptually - it's the next major computing paradigm shift being actively invested in right now, and it will eventually reshape cryptography and simulation.

Steps.Text: Learn what a qubit is and how superposition/entanglement differ from classical bits.
Understand what quantum computers are (and aren't) good at today.
Follow which problems - cryptography, chemistry simulation - are expected to be affected first.
